title: Add confirmation boundary for destructive MCP operations
description:
REASONING: The MCP Unix socket grants AuthContext::Local which bypasses all capability checks. Any same-user process has full admin library control. This is intentional per SECURITY.md (matches macOS same-user trust model), but means local malware/agents can silently delete images, trash collections, or clear selections. We should add a confirmation boundary for destructive operations without breaking the agent workflow for non-destructive ops.

APPROACH:
1. Define destructive operation whitelist: delete_image, trash_image, clear_selections, delete_collection, reset_ratings, purge_embeddings
2. When a non-GUI caller (MCP socket) invokes a destructive op, emit a Tauri event that shows a native macOS confirmation dialog (tauri-plugin-dialog). GUI bypasses this.
3. Add rate-limiting: reject >100 ops/sec from a single MCP connection as an abuse signal.
4. Enrich audit log: record source='mcp-local' vs source='gui' on every write in services/audit.rs.

RISK: Medium — confirmation dialogs could block agent workflows. Solution: make it configurable (opt-in confirmation mode), default off for stdio MCP (trusted CLI), default on for HTTP MCP (remote-capable). Non-destructive MCP ops are never gated.

DEPENDENCY: This relates to imageview-m4w (centralize destructive-operation policy).
design:
TESTING SCENARIOS:
1. Via MCP socket: call list_images (no confirmation) → works
2. Via MCP socket with confirmation on: call delete_image → confirmation dialog appears in GUI
3. Via MCP socket: call delete_image 101x in 1 second → rate limit triggers
4. Via GUI: call delete_image → no confirmation (uses existing trash confirmation flow)
5. Verify audit log shows correct source for each operation
6. Verify Viewer/Curator/Operator/Admin capability checks still work independently of confirmation

PREFLIGHT: npm run preflight:full must pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Destructive operation list is defined and centralized (not scattered)
- [ ] MCP destructive ops trigger confirmation when confirmation mode is enabled
- [ ] GUI operations never trigger confirmation
- [ ] Audit log records source (mcp-local/mcp-http/gui) on all writes
- [ ] Rate limiting rejects >100 ops/sec from single MCP connection
- [ ] Configuration: confirmation mode default off for stdio, on for HTTP
- [ ] Non-destructive MCP ops (list, search, read) never blocked
- [ ] Tests cover: confirmation triggered, rate limit hit, audit logging

title: Add automated browser E2E smoke tests to CI
description:
REASONING: The browser E2E suite (tests/e2e/) exists but is classified as a manual pre-push gate. UI regressions are caught by 91 Vitest unit tests + Rust tests, but not by an automated browser harness. The tauri-mock.ts layer already exists for browser-only testing. This is a quick win — the infrastructure exists, it just needs CI wiring.

APPROACH:
1. Add e2e-smoke job to .github/workflows/ci.yml:
   - Runs on macos-latest (needs Tauri build for mock mode)
   - Builds frontend: npm run build
   - Builds Rust in test mode: cargo build --features test-support
   - Runs tests/e2e/ suite against localhost:1420
   - Uses existing run-e2e.sh or Playwright

2. Keep scope to 5-10 smoke tests:
   - App loads and renders
   - Grid displays images
   - Import via drag-drop simulation
   - Keyboard navigation (arrow keys, rate)
   - Command palette opens
   - Loupe view opens/closes

3. Do NOT try to cover everything in browser E2E — the 91 Vitest tests are the primary safety net.

RISK: Low — infrastructure exists. Main risk is CI flakiness from browser timing; mitigate with generous timeouts and retry.

DEPENDENCY: Should run after frontend + rust jobs pass (needs: [frontend, rust]).
design:
TESTING SCENARIOS:
1. Push a PR → e2e-smoke job runs and passes
2. Introduce a UI regression (break grid rendering) → e2e-smoke fails
3. Verify tauri-mock.ts provides sufficient mock data for smoke tests
4. Verify CI job timeout doesn't kill legitimate slow runs
5. Verify job skips gracefully if Chrome Beta unavailable (fallback to headless Chromium)
6. Check job runtime stays under 5 minutes (adjust scope if needed)

PREFLIGHT: npm run preflight:full must pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] e2e-smoke job added to ci.yml
- [ ] Job runs on PR + push-to-main (same trigger as other jobs)
- [ ] 5-10 core smoke tests automated (app load, grid, import, keyboard, palette)
- [ ] Tests use tauri-mock.ts (NOT api.ts mock — policy compliant)
- [ ] CI job completes in <5 minutes
- [ ] Job has retry logic or generous timeouts for browser flakiness
- [ ] Manual E2E gate (CULL_PREFLIGHT_SKIP_E2E=1) remains for local pre-push
- [ ] Green CI run with all smoke tests passing

title: Benchmark and optimize single SQLite connection bottleneck
description:
REASONING: Database uses a single Arc<Mutex<Connection>>. Under heavy concurrent ML enrichment + import + UI queries, all DB access serializes through one mutex. For a single-user desktop app this is likely fine, but it is a scaling ceiling. We should benchmark before optimizing.

APPROACH (phased):
Phase 1 — Benchmark (must complete before any code change):
  - Add timing instrumentation around conn.lock() hold times in hot paths (import, list_images, embedding writes)
  - Create a test that runs concurrent read + write operations and measures throughput
  - Document findings: are holds consistently <1ms? Where are the outliers?

Phase 2 — If benchmark justifies (holds >5ms regularly):
  - Read-only connection pool: Open N additional read-only connections (SQLite WAL allows concurrent readers). Route SELECT queries through a DbReader that doesn't contend with the write mutex.
  - Batch ML writes: Group embedding/detection results and commit in single transactions instead of per-image inserts. Add flush_results() to jobs.rs.

Phase 3 — If benchmark shows <1ms holds:
  - Close this issue. Document that single-connection is sufficient for desktop workload.

RISK: Low if Phase 1 is done first. Medium if jumping straight to connection pool (SQLite WAL + multiple connections needs careful busy_timeout configuration).
design:
TESTING SCENARIOS:
1. Run benchmark: import 1000 images while simultaneously calling list_images 100x — measure p50/p99 lock hold times
2. If pool implemented: spawn 10 reader tasks + 1 writer task, verify no SQLITE_BUSY errors
3. Verify WAL checkpoint still works (PRAGMA wal_checkpoint)
4. Verify migration backup (VACUUM INTO) works with multiple connections open
5. Manual: import large folder while browsing — UI should not stutter

PREFLIGHT: npm run preflight:full must pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Phase 1: Benchmark report documents conn.lock() hold times across import/list/embed paths
- [ ] Phase 1: Test reproduces concurrent read+write scenario with measured throughput
- [ ] If holds <1ms: issue closed with benchmark documentation
- [ ] If holds >5ms: read-only connection pool implemented and tested
- [ ] If implemented: concurrent read test shows no mutex contention for SELECT queries
- [ ] cargo test passes (including migration safety tests with new connections)
- [ ] No SQLite database lock errors under concurrent load test

title: Reduce api.ts IPC drift via type generation + domain split
description:
REASONING: api.ts is 1597 lines hand-mirroring 229 Rust command signatures. Type drift between TS interfaces and Rust return shapes is only caught by svelte-check (compile-time), not by runtime contract tests for every command. A previous mock layer caused fake-data bugs (documented hard-won lesson). We need type safety without a mock layer.

APPROACH (two complementary fixes):
1. Auto-generate TS types from Rust: Add ts-rs or specta crate to backend. Annotate structs that cross the IPC boundary. Add scripts/gen-api-types.ts step that outputs src/lib/api-types.ts. Wire into preflight quick.

2. Split api.ts by domain to match backend command modules:
   lib/api/
   ├── index.ts        # re-exports, invoke() wrapper
   ├── library.ts      # image listing/get/search
   ├── curation.ts     # rate/decide/select
   ├── collections.ts
   ├── embeddings.ts
   └── import.ts

3. Extend tauri-command-contract.test.ts to assert every #[tauri::command] in Rust has a matching TS export.

RISK: Medium — ts-rs/specta integration with Tauri 2 needs validation. Must not break the no-mock-layer policy.
design:
TESTING SCENARIOS:
1. Generate types, verify all ImageWithFile, Selection, Collection structs have TS equivalents
2. Run tauri-command-contract.test.ts — should pass with full coverage
3. Add a new Rust command WITHOUT updating api.ts → contract test should fail
4. Manual: app loads, grid renders, import works, ratings persist
5. Verify svelte-check catches type mismatches that manual mirroring would miss

PREFLIGHT: npm run preflight:full must pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] api.ts (or api/index.ts) is under 400 lines
- [ ] Generated api-types.ts covers all IPC return types
- [ ] scripts/gen-api-types.ts runs in <5 seconds
- [ ] npm run preflight:quick includes type generation step
- [ ] tauri-command-contract.test.ts asserts coverage for all 229 commands
- [ ] No tauri-mock.ts import from api layer (policy enforced)
- [ ] npm run check passes
- [ ] npm test passes

title: Split mcp/tools.rs (4504 lines, 75 tools) into domain sub-handlers
description:
REASONING: mcp/tools.rs is 4504 lines holding 75 #[tool] methods in a single CullMcp struct. This makes it hard to audit the full tool surface holistically, hard to find specific tools, and hard to review security-relevant path-redaction logic scattered across tools. rmcp supports #[tool] on separate impl blocks on the same struct.

APPROACH: Split into domain sub-handlers, each as impl CullMcp in a separate file:

  mcp/
  ├── tools.rs           # CullMcp struct, handler dispatch, shared context (~300 lines)
  ├── tools/
  │   ├── library.rs     # list_images, get_image, search, count, stats (~15 tools)
  │   ├── curation.rs    # rate, decide, select, undo, tag (~15 tools)
  │   ├── collections.rs # create/update/delete smart+static collections (~10 tools)
  │   ├── embeddings.rs  # generate, search-similar, compare (~10 tools)
  │   ├── import_export.rs # import_folder, export, exchange (~8 tools)
  │   ├── system.rs      # tokens, capabilities, config, health (~10 tools)
  │   └── lineage.rs     # lineage queries, generation runs (~7 tools)

RISK: Low-Medium — rmcp macro behavior on split impls needs verification. Auth/redaction logic must remain centralized in auth.rs, not duplicated.
design:
TESTING SCENARIOS:
1. Count #[tool] attributes before and after — must match (75)
2. Run any existing MCP integration tests
3. Manual: use mcp-stdio bridge to call tools across all domains
4. Verify capability gating still works (Viewer token cannot rate, Curator can)
5. Verify path redaction in error messages still functions

PREFLIGHT: npm run preflight:full must pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] tools.rs is under 500 lines (struct + dispatch only)
- [ ] Each tool sub-module is under 800 lines
- [ ] All 75 tools are accounted for (count verified)
- [ ] AuthContext checks remain in auth.rs (no duplication)
- [ ] cargo test passes — all MCP tool tests pass unchanged
- [ ] Manual: connect via MCP socket, verify tools list_images + rate + create_collection work

title: Linux x64 AppImage beta and deb first target
description:
Deliver the first Linux release target as a deliberately scoped beta: Linux x64, AppImage for direct download/updater testing, and deb for Debian/Ubuntu installation.

This is a child of the broader Linux desktop version epic. Do not expand this target to rpm, Flatpak, Snap, AUR, or Linux arm64 until the baseline is proven and follow-up issues explicitly choose those tracks.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Baseline decision names the build base, minimum supported distro, and validation distros.
- Tauri build produces Linux x64 AppImage and deb artifacts from the chosen baseline or CI runner.

- Required Linux system dependencies are installed and documented.

- AppImage launch smoke passes, and updater/signature behavior is documented or tested.

- deb install smoke passes on the target Ubuntu/Debian baseline.
- Runtime smoke covers import/open library, thumbnails, SQLite persistence, Secret Service behavior, ONNX model load/search, PDF preview, tray/menu behavior, deep links, and file associations where supported.

- macOS-only or unavailable Linux features are exposed as clear limitations, not silent failures.

- Release docs call this a Linux x64 beta and list unsupported Linux items.
acceptance_criteria: Linux x64 beta ships AppImage and deb artifacts from a documented baseline, with install/runtime smoke tests and explicit limitations documented.

title: Enable denied Clippy warnings in Rust CI
description:
Why: The parent issue is not done until CI enforces the strict policy that GitHub issue #1 asks for. This should happen after the backlog is cleared.

Scope: Update the Rust CI/preflight command to run Clippy with -D warnings, document the policy in contributor docs, and confirm the full Rust check path passes.
acceptance_criteria: npm run ci:rust or the equivalent Rust CI script runs cargo clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings; CONTRIBUTING.md documents the strict warning policy; latest GitHub CI run is green with denied Clippy warnings enabled.

title: Clean strict Clippy warnings in Rust test targets
description:
Why: cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings also fails on tests, including bool assert comparisons, useless vec allocations, unnecessary casts, and items_after_test_module.

Scope: Clean test-only warnings without weakening test intent; move helper items before test modules where needed and simplify assertions/data setup.
acceptance_criteria: cargo clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings no longer reports test-target-only warnings; the full Rust lib test suite passes.

title: Resolve lifecycle and resource-management Clippy warnings
description:
Why: Strict Clippy flags lifecycle/resource warnings such as drop_non_drop, zombie_processes, and new_without_default. These can indicate unclear ownership or process management behavior.

Scope: Review preview command state lifetimes, spawned tray process handling, ActionManager/FileWatcher defaults, and related lifecycle warnings; fix behavior or add narrow rationale where intentional.
acceptance_criteria: drop_non_drop, zombie_processes, and new_without_default warnings are resolved or explicitly justified; process-spawn behavior remains correct; affected unit tests pass.

title: Extract complex tuple payload types for catalog and plugin paths
description:
Why: Strict Clippy reports type_complexity in catalog command/CLI/database code and plugin invoke paths. These tuple payloads are hard to read and easy to mis-order.

Scope: Replace repeated large tuple types with named structs or type aliases at the appropriate module boundary, preserving serialization and database behavior.
acceptance_criteria: type_complexity warnings in catalog-related code and plugin invoke paths are resolved or explicitly justified; payload field ordering becomes named or centrally defined; catalog and plugin tests pass.

title: Resolve command and database API shape Clippy lints
description:
Why: Strict Clippy flags too_many_arguments across Tauri commands, database APIs, export/generation services, and audit helpers. These need intentional treatment because some are external IPC/API surfaces.

Scope: Refactor internal multi-argument functions into parameter structs where it reduces complexity, and add narrow allow attributes with rationale where Tauri IPC or stable API shape makes refactoring undesirable.
acceptance_criteria: All too_many_arguments warnings in commands, db_core, preview, and services are either removed through parameter structs or explicitly allowed with a local rationale; no command payload compatibility is broken; relevant Rust tests pass.

title: Fix low-risk mechanical Clippy warnings
description:
Why: The strict Clippy run includes many low-risk warnings that should be fixed directly before tackling larger API-shape work.

Scope: Clean up unused imports/dead code where valid, manual clamp patterns, needless borrows, redundant closures, useless conversions, bool assert comparisons, useless vec allocations, unnecessary casts, and simple map_or/is_some_and suggestions.
acceptance_criteria: cargo clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings no longer reports the low-risk mechanical lint classes covered by this task; behavior remains unchanged; focused tests or existing Rust tests pass for touched modules.

title: Inventory strict Clippy backlog and choose allow/refactor policy
description:
Why: Strict Clippy currently reports a broad backlog, and some warnings are mechanical while others are public API or Tauri IPC shape decisions. This task creates the baseline and decision policy for the cleanup.

Scope: Re-run cargo clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings, group warnings by lint and module, and record which lints should be fixed versus explicitly allowed with rationale.
acceptance_criteria: A fresh strict Clippy run is summarized by lint/module; API-shape lints such as too_many_arguments have an explicit fix-or-allow policy; child cleanup tasks can reference the baseline.

title: Add Linux x64 CI release artifacts
description:
Add Linux x64 packaging to the release workflow after the Linux support baseline is decided and runtime portability checks pass.

Current release workflow only builds macOS Intel and Apple Silicon. Tauri’s GitHub guide supports tauri-action-based Linux builds and notes workflows can build Linux x64/arm64 artifacts, while Cull’s existing cross-platform distribution doc already recommends staging Linux x64 after dependency/install smoke tests.

Start with x64 AppImage plus deb; add rpm once Fedora-family validation is passing. Keep GitHub releases draft until install smoke tests pass.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Release workflow has a Linux x64 matrix entry using the chosen baseline runner/container.
- Required apt/system dependencies are installed.

- npm/Rust checks run before packaging.

- AppImage and deb artifacts are produced with updater signatures.

- Draft-release behavior remains.

- Artifact smoke-test steps are documented or automated.
acceptance_criteria: Release workflow has a Linux x64 matrix entry using the chosen baseline runner/container; required apt/system dependencies are installed; npm/Rust checks run before packaging; AppImage and deb artifacts are produced with updater signatures; draft-release behavior remains; artifact smoke-test steps are documented or automated.

title: Audit Linux runtime portability and feature gaps
description:
Audit and fix Linux runtime portability before shipping Linux artifacts.

Known current gaps from code inspection: OCR uses Apple Vision and returns unsupported off macOS; image clipboard paste/copy, Open With discovery, and system sharing return macOS-only errors; platform-only decodable formats such as HEIC/AVIF/SVG/JXL/PSD are gated to macOS; keyring uses Secret Service on Linux and needs install/runtime validation; ONNX Runtime downloaded binaries and pdfium-render need Linux smoke coverage.

Decide which gaps are acceptable documented limitations for the first Linux release and which require implementation before artifacts are published.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Linux build compiles in CI or a documented Linux environment.
- macOS-only features show clear unsupported states or portable implementations.

- Secret Service/keyring behavior is validated.

- ONNX and PDF rendering smoke tests pass on the chosen Linux baseline.

- README/docs list Linux-specific limitations.
acceptance_criteria: Linux build compiles in CI or a documented Linux environment; macOS-only features show clear unsupported states or portable implementations; Secret Service/keyring behavior is validated; ONNX and PDF rendering smoke tests pass on the chosen Linux baseline; README/docs list Linux-specific limitations.

title: Research Linux support matrix and package strategy
description:
Research and decide Cull’s first Linux support matrix before enabling public Linux release artifacts.

Source-backed constraints: Tauri 2 Linux builds require WebKitGTK 4.1 and related system packages; Tauri AppImage docs recommend building on the oldest base system we intend to support because glibc compatibility is forward-compatible, not backward-compatible; AppImage is easiest for direct download but deb/rpm matter for package-manager installs.

Deliver a short decision note covering target distros, minimum baseline, x64 vs arm64, first package formats, updater behavior, and unsupported/known-limited platform features.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Decision note names the first supported Linux baseline and validation distros.
- Package format order is explicit.

- Build host/container choice is justified.

- Updater/static latest.json implications are covered.

- Links to official Tauri docs and Cull distribution docs are included.
acceptance_criteria: Decision note names the first supported Linux baseline and validation distros; package format order is explicit; build host/container choice is justified; updater/static latest.json implications are covered; links to official Tauri docs and Cull distribution docs are included.

title: Linux desktop version
description:
Plan and implement a first-class Linux version of Cull, including Tauri 2 runtime support, packaging, dependency/runtime compatibility, CI/build automation, release artifacts, and documentation.

Initial scope should answer which Linux distributions and package formats Cull will support, how image/ML dependencies behave outside macOS, what CI runners and signing/checksum steps are required, and what user-facing install/update path is acceptable for an open-source desktop release.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Linux support has a documented target distro/package matrix.

- Required Tauri/system dependencies are identified and tested.
- CI can produce Linux artifacts.

- Release docs explain installation and known limitations.

- Follow-up child issues cover packaging, runtime compatibility, test coverage, and release automation.
acceptance_criteria: Linux support has a documented target distro/package matrix; required Tauri/system dependencies are identified and tested; CI can produce Linux artifacts; release docs explain installation and known limitations; follow-up child issues cover packaging, runtime compatibility, test coverage, and release automation.
notes: Research snapshot 2026-06-17: official Tauri docs list Linux distribution formats as AppImage, Debian, RPM, Snap, Flatpak, and AUR; AppImage is easiest for direct download but must be built on the oldest supported base because glibc compatibility breaks backward; Tauri Linux development/build dependencies include WebKitGTK 4.1, libxdo, OpenSSL, appindicator, librsvg, and standard build tooling. Cull already documents Linux x64 as next/medium effort, but the release workflow currently builds macOS only. Code inspection found Linux limitations for Apple Vision OCR, image clipboard, Open With, system sharing, platform-only image decoding, Secret Service keyring validation, ONNX Runtime, and PDFium smoke coverage.

title: Validate gesture support on Mac hardware
description: Validate gesture support on real Mac trackpad and Magic Mouse hardware. Confirm unmodified scroll never zooms, modifier-wheel zoom works, Loupe swipe navigation does not trigger while panning, Canvas pan/zoom does not fight item/crop edits, and decide whether a minimal macOS native bridge is needed for true smart zoom/pinch gaps.
acceptance_criteria: Decision recorded: web events sufficient or native bridge issue created

title: Implement PDF media-format path and catalogization migration
description: Implement PDF-first-class media import and catalog support from research phase. Add media-layer scaffolding where practical: stable PDF thumbnail + preview import path, catalog indexing for PDFs without flattening into image rows, background job guards, and deterministic export/render handling. Keep ImageWithFile/API compatibility in v1.
acceptance_criteria: User can import PDFs from folder/files and get entries with format 'pdf' and generated preview thumbnail. PDFs are excluded from image-only ML quality/detection workflows by default unless explicitly configured. Export and preview surfaces should use generated preview path, not raw PDF bytes. Add tests for import, job filtering, and export manifest source path behavior.
close_reason: Implemented PDF media catalogization path with media_assets/media_files/pdf_pages migrations, PDF import thumbnails/previews, image-only job filtering, preview/export routing, catalog/RBAC command surface, and tests. Verified with npm run check, npm test, cargo test -q, and pre-push full tier.

title: Support PDF import, catalogization, and media-format handling
description: Add first-class PDF support to Cull: PDFs can be imported, catalogued, previewed, searched, and represented as media assets without breaking existing image workflows. Scope includes database/media-model design, import pipeline, PDF thumbnails/previews, metadata/text extraction, security/licensing policy, frontend viewing UX, and phased implementation tasks.
design: Research recommends PDFium/pdfium-render for import-time thumbnails/page metadata, optional pure-Rust text helpers after a spike, PDF.js only for interactive reading, and a phased media_assets/media_files/pdf_pages schema path that preserves existing ImageWithFile APIs during phase 1.
acceptance_criteria: PDF support is decomposed into researched, testable implementation tasks with clear schema, import, preview, search, and UI boundaries

title: Research PDF import and catalogization support
description: Research how Cull should support PDF as an importable/catalogued media format. Cover PDF ingestion scope, page preview/thumbnail strategy, metadata extraction, text/OCR/search implications, database/media-model changes, frontend viewing UX, export/import interactions, licensing/security constraints, and recommended Rust/Svelte/Tauri tech stack. Deliver a document with implementation options, trade-offs, and a recommended path before code implementation.
acceptance_criteria: Doc proposes phased implementation tasks suitable for bd epics/tasks
close_reason: Research complete. Documented PDF import/catalogization tech stack at docs/research/2026-06-16-pdf-import-catalogization-tech-stack.md, with recommendation for PDFium/pdfium-render, media_assets/media_files/pdf_pages schema, parent-PDF curation in v1, security/licensing constraints, and phased bd implementation tasks.

title: Design Google Drive asset delivery plugin
description: Follow-up plugin idea separate from the generative art catalog layer. Research and design a Google Drive integration for safe asset delivery/sync/export workflows, consuming Cull catalog metadata without being part of the v1 catalog schema.
acceptance_criteria: Plugin consumes catalog metadata through whitelisted core commands rather than direct database access

title: Document the agent surface: headless_cli usage and agent_snapshots demo loop
description:
When the identity is agent-native, I need the two category-unique proofs — headless_cli reusing the MCP tool layer via call_tool, and agent_snapshots/display-ui view-driving — documented for launch, so the differentiator is demonstrable by a stranger with a token, not just implemented.

Track B, Day 4

Test plan: Pure-docs issue — verification command instead of a failing test: each CLI invocation in the doc is executed verbatim against a dev build and exits 0 (transcript attached to the issue); `test -f docs/agents.md` (or the chosen location) passes; a reviewer replays at least the token-create + call_tool + snapshot sequence from the doc alone.

## Acceptance Criteria
Docs (README section or docs/agents.md) cover: creating a scoped token, calling tools via the headless CLI, capturing an annotated snapshot, and driving selection in the live UI; every documented command was executed verbatim during writing; the end-to-end demo loop from the judge's keep-anyway list is sketched as the launch demo.
acceptance_criteria: Docs (README section or docs/agents.md) cover: creating a scoped token, calling tools via the headless CLI, capturing an annotated snapshot, and driving selection in the live UI; every documented command was executed verbatim during writing; the end-to-end demo loop from the judge's keep-anyway list is sketched as the launch demo.
close_reason: Closed

title: mcp_settings + privacy_dashboard polish: expiry UX and audit visibility on the headline config surface
description:
When the McpSettings panel is the first screen an agent-curious user opens and the privacy dashboard is where they verify 'scoped, audited, revocable', I need token-expiry controls and failed-auth/audit visibility surfaced in the UI so the trust claims are inspectable, not just stored.

Track B, Day 4

Test plan: Write failing component tests first: src/lib/components/mcp-settings-expiry.test.ts asserting the create-token form renders an expiry input and the resulting create call passes expires_at (fails — no such control exists), and src/lib/components/privacy-dashboard-audit.test.ts asserting mocked _auth_failed audit rows render in the dashboard list. `npx vitest run src/lib/components/mcp-settings-expiry.test.ts src/lib/components/privacy-dashboard-audit.test.ts`.

## Acceptance Criteria
Token-create flow in McpSettings.svelte exposes an optional expiry; token list shows expiry status; privacy dashboard surfaces recent audit entries including the new _auth_failed rows; partial-coverage gap on McpSettings narrowed with at least the new flows under test.
acceptance_criteria: Token-create flow in McpSettings.svelte exposes an optional expiry; token list shows expiry status; privacy dashboard surfaces recent audit entries including the new _auth_failed rows; partial-coverage gap on McpSettings narrowed with at least the new flows under test.
close_reason: Closed

title: Upgrade Vite/SvelteKit before merging vite-plugin-svelte 7
description: The remote branch origin/dependabot/npm_and_yarn/sveltejs/vite-plugin-svelte-7.1.2 bumps @sveltejs/vite-plugin-svelte to 7.1.2, whose peer dependency requires Vite ^8.0.0-beta.7 || ^8.0.0. The app currently uses Vite ^6.0.3 and the installed SvelteKit peer range supports Vite 5/6/7-beta, so merging that branch alone creates an invalid dependency graph. Treat it as a framework upgrade: update Vite/SvelteKit/plugin together, refresh package-lock, run full preflight/build, then delete the stale Dependabot branch.
acceptance_criteria: origin/dependabot/npm_and_yarn/sveltejs/vite-plugin-svelte-7.1.2 is merged or closed after the compatible upgrade

title: Decide & integrate GitHub-based update checks vs tauri plugin-updater
description:
Salvaged from stale branch codex/db-encryption (misnamed; 97 commits behind, not merged). Reference commit: eae987c04 'Add GitHub update checks' — adds src/lib/update-manager.ts (+124, +144 test, +42 contract test), a 'Check for Updates' native menu action (menu id check_update), an auto-update preference toggle in McpSettings, and REWRITES UpdateBanner.svelte from @tauri-apps/plugin-updater's check() to a GitHub-releases-API checker.

JTBD: As a user, I want the app to tell me when a new version is available and let me trigger a manual check.

Why deferred (product decision needed): main currently ships a working UpdateBanner built on tauri plugin-updater. The branch REPLACES that mechanism with a GitHub-releases checker — an architecture swap, not an additive feature. Decide which mechanism to ship (plugin-updater needs the signed-release pipeline that was blocked on GitHub billing; the GitHub-API checker is self-contained but unsigned). Then integrate update-manager.ts + the check_update menu action cleanly, and merge the McpSettings auto-update toggle into main's current settings panel (note: branch's settings destructuring is stale and drops clipboardPasteDateFormatSetting).

Acceptance criteria:
- One update mechanism chosen and documented.
- 'Check for Updates' menu action wired (check_update) calling checkForUpdates().
- Auto-update preference toggle merged into the CURRENT McpSettings settings array (do not drop existing settings).
- update-manager unit + contract tests pass.
- Reference eae987c04 for behavior; do not cherry-pick the stale UpdateBanner rewrite blindly.

Note: the branch's other 'database encryption' commit (7e09bbe89) only added a bd issue stub, no implementation.
close_reason: Merged from origin/codex/preview-display-window-controls on codex/integrate-preview-display-remaining; npm run check and npm test pass after conflict resolution.

title: Reimplement experimental PDF preview-on-import against current architecture
description:
Salvaged from stale branch codex/canvas-view-upgrade (commit a1a401a78, 219 commits behind main, not merged).

The branch added experimental PDF import previews: PDFs imported and thumbnailed, with viewers (Compare/Loupe/Tinder) rendering the rendered preview via usesRenderedPreview()/RENDERED_PREVIEW_EXTENSIONS. It could NOT be cherry-picked: it predates main's safeAssetPreviewPath() viewer-safety layer (taking it would regress that) and conflicts across 8 points in db_core/import.rs which main has since rewritten.

JTBD: As a user with AI-generated PDFs, I want to import them and see page previews so I can curate them alongside images.

Acceptance criteria:
- PDF files are accepted by import and produce a rendered thumbnail/preview.
- Viewers render the PDF preview via the existing safeAssetPreviewPath() path (do NOT bypass it).
- usesRenderedPreview()/RENDERED_PREVIEW_EXTENSIONS extended to include 'pdf'.
- Implemented fresh against current import.rs/thumbnails.rs/extensions.rs (reference, do not cherry-pick, a1a401a78).
- E2E preview-decode coverage as in the branch's test commits (c9f210d9f, b3b6b9b23, 4ab9efbaf).

title: Keep model, asset, and AI-provenance audits current
description: Category: AuditNeeded. Evidence: OPEN_SOURCE_AUDIT.md records Apache-2.0 alignment, dep license checks, AI-authorship policy, model-weight boundaries, disabled YOLO/NudeNet, asset inventory. Problem: good for this snapshot but model cards/deps/assets/provenance drift. Recommendation: make license/provenance review a required release artifact, esp. when adding models/assets/AI code. JTBD: When Cull ships publicly, I want source/model/asset rights documented.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Release checklist requires updated license/provenance audit notes
- [ ] New model downloads require source URL, SPDX/license, checksum, commercial-use, attribution
- [ ] Asset additions require documented ownership/permission before merge

title: Promote keyboard and accessibility E2E coverage into automation
description: Category: BestPractices. Evidence: AGENTS.md classifies browser E2E (Chrome Beta/CDP) as a manual pre-push gate; package.json exposes test:e2e. Problem: manual gates easy to skip. Recommendation: automated smoke coverage for command palette, grid keyboard nav, destructive dialogs, import empty states, core a11y in CI/nightly. JTBD: When UI behavior changes, I want keyboard/a11y regressions caught automatically.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] CI/nightly runs headless/browser smoke for grid, palette, dialogs, static publishing
- [ ] Suite includes keyboard-only nav/selection/rating/command/dialog-cancel flows
- [ ] A11y checks fail on missing dialog roles, unlabeled controls, broken focus return

title: Add fuzz and property tests for untrusted parsers
description: Category: AuditNeeded. Evidence: deeplink paths canonicalized; smart-collection JSON -> recursive filter tree -> SQL; source detection parses filenames/metadata via regex. Problem: attacker/user-controlled parsers; only unit examples exist. Recommendation: cargo-fuzz/proptest targets for deeplinks, smart-filter JSON, sidecar metadata, source detection, static export patch/manifest. JTBD: When Cull parses external strings/metadata, I want pathological input tested automatically.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Fuzz/property targets exist for deeplink, smart filters, source detection, sidecar/manifest
- [ ] CI or nightly runs a bounded fuzz/property suite
- [ ] Any parser panic on arbitrary input is a failing test

title: Run an MCP remote-access threat-model and pentest
description: Category: AuditNeeded. Evidence: SECURITY.md: local MCP socket is full-admin by filesystem perms; HTTP uses bearer tokens/roles/Origin/localhost; mcp-remote-access.md documents Tailscale/Cloudflare/ngrok; mcp/http.rs has body/rate/concurrency limits + timeouts. Problem: high-power automation surface needs adversarial testing before remote-use marketing. Recommendation: focused threat model: token theft/replay/brute force, role/scope bypass, Origin behind tunnels, audit-log integrity, local-socket abuse. JTBD: When I expose MCP beyond localhost, I want the control plane tested adversarially.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Negative tests cover missing/invalid/expired/revoked tokens for every HTTP MCP route
- [ ] Role/scope bypass attempts tested against all MCP tools
- [ ] Written threat model documents boundaries, assets, attackers, mitigations, residual risk

title: Run a dedicated privacy and data-flow audit
description: Category: AuditNeeded. Evidence: PRIVACY.md lists BYOK cloud data sent to Gemini/OpenAI/OpenRouter/remote Ollama/MCP HTTP and notes provider terms need review. Problem: useful doc but not a formal DPIA/data-flow audit. Recommendation: dedicated privacy audit covering consent gates, provider terms, retention, audit-log prompt storage, clipboard capture, thumbnails, MCP HTTP, GDPR/EU transfer. JTBD: When I enable cloud/remote features, I want to know exactly what leaves my machine.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Data-flow diagram maps every image/prompt/API-key path UI->storage/network
- [ ] Cloud feature UI includes provider-specific consent + sensitive-image warnings
- [ ] Audit-log retention/redaction/export/delete documented and tested

title: Add CVE and SBOM audits to release gates
description: Category: AuditNeeded. Evidence: package.json has audit:licenses but no dependency-vuln/SBOM script; OPEN_SOURCE_AUDIT.md covers licenses not CVEs. Problem: known-vuln exposure not a first-class release gate. Recommendation: cargo audit/deny, npm vuln policy, Dependabot, SBOM for release artifacts. JTBD: When I cut a release, I want dependency vulns and provenance captured.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Release preflight runs Cargo + npm vuln checks with documented severity policy
- [ ] CI produces/validates an SBOM for packaged builds
- [ ] Dependency exceptions require expiry dates + rationale

title: Narrow broad opener permissions
description: Category: Security. Evidence: tauri-command-contract.test.ts expects opener:allow-open-path to allow $HOME/**/* and $APPDATA/**/* though intended only for generated share files. Problem: broader than use case; compromised renderer could open arbitrary $HOME files. Recommendation: restrict opener to export/output dirs; route broader Reveal/Open via validated Rust commands. JTBD: When Cull opens generated files, I want the permission to cover only generated outputs.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] opener:allow-open-path no longer grants all $HOME/**/*
- [ ] Generated export/share files still open successfully
- [ ] Opening unrelated home-dir paths via opener fails in tests

title: Add design-token and contrast regression checks
description: Category: Accessibility. Evidence: AGENTS.md mandates Tokyo Night tokens, no hardcoded colors; Canvas.svelte has hardcoded rgba() overlays/shadows; no systematic contrast enforcement. Problem: visual/contrast regressions creep in via hardcoded colors. Recommendation: style lint/tests allowing hardcoded colors only in token defs/static templates/documented exceptions. JTBD: When the UI changes, I want color/contrast checked automatically.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Component CSS hardcoded colors rejected unless allowlisted
- [ ] Contrast tests cover primary/secondary/disabled/warning/error/focus/selected
- [ ] Canvas crop overlays use named tokens or documented exceptions with contrast tests

title: Replace direct Svelte prop mutation in thumbnails
description: Category: BestPractices. Evidence: Thumbnail.svelte::handleImgError mutates item.thumbnail_path directly on prop-derived object. Problem: direct nested prop mutation is fragile in Svelte 5, can cause stale UI. Recommendation: emit thumbnail-regenerated event or update central images store by id (unidirectional flow). JTBD: When a thumbnail regenerates after load failure, I want parent state updated consistently.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Thumbnail.svelte no longer mutates item/nested prop fields
- [ ] Unit test verifies regeneration updates the parent store and rerenders
- [ ] svelte check + tests pass, no prop-mutation warnings/stale preview regressions

title: Audit P2 — Medium / nice-to-have
description: ChatGPT 5.5 Pro repo audit (2026-06-03). Medium recommendations. See docs/cull-audit-2026-06-03.md.

title: Clean up Clippy warnings before denying warnings in Rust CI
description:
## Why
`cargo clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings` currently fails on the Rust tree with many pre-existing Clippy warnings. Enabling warning denial now would make `npm run ci:rust` fail before the warning cleanup lands.

## Scope
Clean up or explicitly allow the current Clippy warnings, then switch the Rust CI policy from report-only warnings to denied warnings.
acceptance_criteria: `cargo clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings` passes locally; `npm run ci:rust` denies Clippy warnings; CONTRIBUTING.md documents the strict policy.

title: Create a one-command session landing script
description:
## Why
AGENTS.md has a strict landing-plane protocol: file follow-up issues, run gates if code changed, update bd, pull/rebase, sync/commit beads, push, and verify. This is too important to leave as a manual checklist in every session.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A script or documented command runs the landing checks in order and fails loudly on incomplete state.
- It handles current bd capabilities (`bd vc`/`bd dolt`) without assuming unavailable `bd sync`.
- It prints the final git branch/upstream status and bd VC status.
- It never deletes or resets user data and uses `trash` for cleanup if cleanup is needed.
acceptance_criteria: One command performs or verifies the landing protocol; it handles bd command variants; it reports final git/bd status.
close_reason: Added scripts/land-session.sh and npm run land to verify clean worktree, run full/release preflight, handle bd sync fallback via bd vc/Dolt status, pull/rebase, push, and print final git/bd status without deleting or resetting files.

title: Pin developer toolchain and dependency update automation
description:
## Why
The repo documents Rust 1.78+ and Node 20+, but has no `.node-version`, `.nvmrc`, or `rust-toolchain.toml`. `bd version` also warns that two `bd` binaries are on PATH. There is no Dependabot/Renovate config for npm, Cargo, or GitHub Actions updates.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Node and Rust versions are pinned or documented with a deliberate no-pin rationale.
- The duplicate `bd` binary warning is resolved or documented in setup troubleshooting.
- Dependency update automation is configured for npm, Cargo, and GitHub Actions, or explicitly deferred with a reason.
- Update automation respects the license/model audit gate.
acceptance_criteria: Toolchain version policy is encoded; duplicate bd binary warning is addressed; dependency update automation exists or is deliberately deferred.

title: Create a Cull dev workflow skill or agent runbook
description:
## Why
Cull has important agent-specific rules in AGENTS.md: protect `cull.db`, no mock layer in app code, use Svelte 5 runes, use browser E2E patterns, and land work by pushing. These are easy to miss in long sessions and could be better packaged as a reusable workflow skill/runbook.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A Cull dev workflow skill or runbook exists and is easy for agents to invoke.
- It covers bd prime/onboarding, safety rules, quality-gate tiers, E2E patterns, and session close/push protocol.
- It references AGENTS.md as the source of truth instead of duplicating stale details.
- The skill/runbook is versioned in the appropriate skills repo or project docs.
acceptance_criteria: Cull-specific workflow skill/runbook exists; it covers safety, bd, gates, E2E, and landing protocol; it links back to AGENTS.md.

title: Normalize open bd issues with acceptance criteria
description:
## Why
`bd lint` reports 32 open issues missing `## Acceptance Criteria`. This makes agent handoffs underspecified and increases the chance of closing issues based on vibes instead of testable outcomes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every open non-chore issue either has acceptance criteria/success criteria or is explicitly exempted.
- `bd lint` reports zero warnings for open issues.
- New issue creation uses validation or templates that discourage missing acceptance criteria.
acceptance_criteria: Open issue lint warnings are cleared or exempted; new issue creation discourages missing acceptance criteria.

title: Define E2E smoke-test tier and trigger policy
description:
## Why
Cull has browser-only E2E smoke coverage in `tests/e2e/run-e2e.sh`, but `npm run ci` and GitHub CI do not run it. The project also documents Chrome Beta/agent-browser patterns, so contributors need a clear rule for when E2E is required.

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E is classified as local-only, pre-push, nightly, or CI-on-change with a clear rationale.
- The trigger policy names the file areas that require E2E smoke testing.
- The runner works with the selected policy and documents required browser dependencies.
- PR template and AGENTS.md refer to the same E2E rule.
acceptance_criteria: E2E trigger policy is documented; runner supports the chosen tier; PR template and AGENTS.md agree on when E2E is required.

title: Add production build and stricter Rust dependency checks to CI
description:
## Why
Current CI runs Svelte check, Vitest, Rust fmt/clippy/tests, but not `npm run build`. Rust commands also do not explicitly use locked dependency resolution, while the license audit already does. This leaves production build and lockfile drift risks to be caught later.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Frontend CI includes `npm run build` or a documented reason it is intentionally excluded.
- Rust CI uses locked dependency resolution where supported.
- The team decides whether Clippy warnings should be denied and encodes that decision.
- `scripts/check-ci.sh`, GitHub Actions, and `CONTRIBUTING.md` stay aligned.
acceptance_criteria: Frontend production build is covered or explicitly waived; Rust locked-dependency policy is encoded; CI script/docs are aligned.

title: Preview Display: configurable info rail and presets
description:
Add configurable display information for workflows where the external viewer should show more than the image. Start with presets and field toggles rather than a full layout editor.

Candidate presets: Image Only, Client Review, Metadata Review. Candidate fields: filename/title, rating, decision, selected status, dimensions/format, source/provider/model, prompt preview, keywords/tags, generation metadata, and warning/status badges.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
- User can switch between at least Image Only and one info-bearing preset.
- User can choose which supported metadata fields appear in the info rail.
- Settings persist across app restarts.
- Info rail is adjustable in side and width/text size within simple bounded controls.
- Long prompt/keyword text truncates cleanly and never overlaps the image.
notes: Partial implementation in this branch: Image Only, Client Review, and Metadata Review presets are implemented and persisted; overlay state supports filename/rating/decision/metadata rail fields. Remaining work: field-level UI toggles beyond presets plus bounded rail side/width/text-size controls.
close_reason: Implemented persistent presets, field-level View menu toggles, bounded rail side/width/text-size controls, and truncating info rail rendering for native and web Preview Display.

title: Preview Display: histogram and analysis panel integration
description:
Integrate histogram/analysis panels into Preview Display after Cull has a real histogram data path. Do not fake a histogram from unrelated color metrics. The panel should be optional and suitable for reviewer-facing display next to the image.

This issue should coordinate with the existing histogram work item imageview-ce2.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
- Preview Display can show an optional histogram panel using the same canonical histogram data as Loupe/analysis views.
- Histogram panel can be disabled independently from other metadata fields.
- Panel updates when the focused image changes and freezes with the displayed image when Preview Display is frozen.
- RAW/thumbnail fallback behavior is documented if histogram source differs from displayed source.
- This issue does not duplicate the core histogram implementation tracked by imageview-ce2.
notes: Blocked by imageview-ce2. Preview Display deliberately does not fake histogram data from color metrics; docs call out that histogram display waits for the canonical Loupe/analysis histogram path.
close_reason: Preview Display now uses the canonical histogram command, optional histogram field toggle, native/web rendering, freeze-safe shared state, and documented RAW/thumbnail fallback behavior.

title: Preview Display: monitor selection and fullscreen placement
description:
Add external display targeting for the Preview Display. The common use case is an iPad acting as a Mac external display via Sidecar/AirPlay, but the implementation should work with any monitor returned by Tauri.

Do not rely on monitor index. Use monitor metadata/resolution/name where available, persist a resilient preference, and recover gracefully when the target display is disconnected.
acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance criteria:
- User can move Preview Display to a chosen monitor/display.
- Preview Display can enter fullscreen or simple-fullscreen presentation mode on the chosen display.
- Reopening the display attempts to use the saved target monitor when available.
- If the saved monitor is unavailable, Cull opens the display safely on the current/default monitor and tells the user.
- Implementation documents macOS Sidecar limitations: latency/compression/disconnects and non-reference color.
notes: Claude challenge: monitor selection/fullscreen should stay out of the P1 MVP. Native macOS fullscreen creates a separate Space and can be jarring for external display presentation; if this issue adds clean-display mode, prefer evaluating a fake fullscreen approach with decorationless window sized to the monitor.
close_reason: Implemented Preview Display foundation, native window lifecycle, focus sync, dedicated read-only display surface, freeze/blank/presets, monitor placement/fullscreen, focused verification coverage, and tokenized one-way web stream. Verified with npm test, npm run check, focused Rust tests, and local Tauri release build.

title: Clarify destructive, privacy-sensitive, and automation copy
description:
Audit severity: P2 Minor.

Location: src/lib/components/Sidebar.svelte:167, src/lib/components/Sidebar.svelte:528, src/lib/components/Sidebar.svelte:545, src/lib/components/Sidebar.svelte:584, src/lib/components/McpSettings.svelte:389, src/lib/components/StaticPublishingSettings.svelte:250, and src/lib/components/StaticPublishingSettings.svelte:320.

Finding: several labels are terse or ambiguous in high-stakes contexts. Examples: Delete folder and its unique images from library relies on a native confirm; HTTP server copy mentions remote opt-in and scoped tokens without saying where to configure them; AI model states use external, manual, and offline without next action; Search engines toggles between INDEX and NOINDEX without explaining publication consequences; Move Folder and Publish lack object/context in the button label.

Impact: users may hesitate or make mistakes when an action affects local data, privacy, remote access, indexing, or publishing. Cull is local-first and privacy-aware, so copy should make consequences and next steps explicit without sounding alarmist.

Recommendation: audit settings, sidebar, static publishing, and AI model copy for command clarity. Prefer labels that include object plus outcome, short helper text for irreversible or privacy-sensitive actions, and app-native confirmation dialogs where the consequence needs more than one sentence.
acceptance_criteria: Destructive, privacy-sensitive, and publishing actions explain what changes and where; terse states such as external/manual/offline gain actionable helper text; static publishing indexing copy states whether generated pages should be discoverable; native confirm copy is replaced or wrapped where consequence clarity is insufficient.
close_reason: Implemented audit UX/accessibility/copy fixes with failing-first contract coverage; npm run check, npm test, and npm run build pass.

title: Add narrow-window and target-size pass for dense controls
description:
Audit severity: P2 Minor.

Location: src-tauri/tauri.conf.json:16, src/routes/+page.svelte:305, src/lib/components/McpSettings.svelte:598, src/lib/components/EmbeddingExplorer.svelte:2149, src/lib/components/CommandBar.svelte:575, src/lib/components/RuleBuilder.svelte:383, and src/lib/components/TabBar.svelte:142.

Finding: the app opens at 1200x800 but does not define a minimum window size, while the UI relies on fixed desktop dimensions such as a 220px sidebar, 520px settings panel, 240px embedding panel, 180px API inputs, 22 to 28px icon buttons, 16px chip remove buttons, and a 10px range thumb. Only a few components have narrow-width breakpoints.

Impact: users who resize the macOS window, use larger text, or operate on smaller displays can hit clipped panels, crowded tabs, and small pointer targets. Even for a desktop-first app, repeated controls under 24px are hard to operate and easy to miss.

WCAG: 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum where applicable, plus responsive usability.

Recommendation: either set an explicit app minWidth/minHeight that matches the designed desktop surface, or add breakpoints for the app shell, settings panel, embedding panel, command bar, and dense chips. Normalize minimum pointer target sizes for repeated controls.
acceptance_criteria: At the configured minimum window size, settings, command bar, sidebar, embedding explorer, and rule chips do not horizontally overflow; repeated pointer targets are at least 24px and primary controls are closer to 32 to 44px; document or configure the app min window size; add a responsive smoke test or screenshot check.
close_reason: Implemented audit UX/accessibility/copy fixes with failing-first contract coverage; npm run check, npm test, and npm run build pass.

title: Remove non-token gradients, side-stripe toasts, and decorative glass
description:
Audit severity: P2 Minor.

Location: src/lib/components/Toast.svelte:49, src/lib/components/CommandBar.svelte:538, src/lib/components/CommandBar.svelte:686, src/lib/components/CommandBar.svelte:724, src/lib/components/McpSettings.svelte:879, src/lib/components/RuleBuilder.svelte:343, and src/lib/components/EmbeddingExplorer.svelte:2608.

Finding: product chrome has drifted from the documented token system and from the impeccable design laws. Toast variants use colored border-left stripes greater than 1px. CommandBar and several controls use decorative linear gradients and hard-coded rgba mixes. EmbeddingExplorer inspector uses backdrop-filter blur that reads as glass by default.

Impact: the app starts to feel like several visual systems at once, and future theme changes become fragile. The side-stripe toast pattern and decorative glass/gradient treatment are explicitly banned by the design audit standard.

Recommendation: express status through full borders, icons, text, or tokenized background tints rather than side stripes. Replace decorative gradients and raw rgba values with named tokens or color-mix usages tied to root tokens. Keep blur only if it is necessary for legibility over canvas content and document that exception.
